skill.name: meta-radial-plot
skill.description: Generate radial plots (Radial Plot/Galbraith Plot) for heterogeneity analysis. Visually assess heterogeneity across studies by displaying the relationship between standardized effect sizes and precision. Input: Meta-analysis data in CSV format; Output: Radial plot PNG and data CSV.
skill.summary: Automates radial plot generation for meta-analysis heterogeneity assessment from CSV data.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 46 static findings are false positives. The 'weak cryptographic algorithm' alerts matched the abbreviation 'CI' (confidence interval) in statistical text. 'System reconnaissance' alerts matched data validation error messages. 'Shell backtick execution' alerts matched markdown code formatting in documentation. The 'hardcoded URL' is the repository source link. The 'os file operations' alert matches legitimate directory creation for output files. This is a valid meta-analysis data visualization skill with no malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptographic algorithm pattern - false positive
low_findings.description: Scanner matched 'CI' in '95% confidence interval' and similar statistical terms as cryptographic algorithm references. No actual crypto algorithms (MD5, SHA1, DES, AES) appear in any file. All matches are meta-analysis statistical terminology.

low_findings.title: System reconnaissance pattern - false positive
low_findings.description: Scanner matched data validation error messages and quality guidance text. Lines flagged contain 'at least 3 studies are required' and similar input validation. These are standard data integrity checks, not system reconnaissance.

low_findings.title: Shell backtick execution pattern - false positive
low_findings.description: Scanner matched backtick characters ` in SKILL.md which are standard Markdown formatting for inline code and fenced code blocks. No Ruby backtick execution is present. All matches are documentation examples showing bash commands and script references.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL pattern - false positive
low_findings.description: URL is the GitHub source repository link in the skill header attribution. This is standard open-source attribution, not a network call or data exfiltration endpoint.

low_findings.title: Python os file operations - false positive
low_findings.description: os.makedirs(self.output_dir, exist_ok=True) creates an output directory for saving generated plot files. This is standard and necessary behavior for a script that produces file outputs. No deletion, modification, or unauthorized access occurs.

dangerous_patterns.title: Validated filesystem write operations
dangerous_patterns.description: Script creates output directories and saves PNG/CSV files using os.makedirs and matplotlib savefig. These are expected operations for a plot-generation skill. Paths come from user-provided CLI arguments and are validated before use.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command execution instructions
dangerous_patterns.description: SKILL.md documents instructions for the AI to run Rscript and Python scripts via shell commands. All command templates reference scripts within the skill package. Input paths are validated before execution per the documented workflow.

content.user_title: Generate Radial Plots for Meta-Analysis Heterogeneity
content.value_statement: This skill generates radial plots (Galbraith plots) for meta-analysis heterogeneity assessment. It automates the creation of publication-ready statistical plots from CSV data without requiring manual R or Python coding.
actual_capabilities: Generate radial plots (Galbraith plots) from CSV meta-analysis data
Support binary, continuous, and survival data types with automatic detection
Calculate heterogeneity statistics including I-squared, Tau-squared, and Q statistic
Identify studies outside the 95% confidence band with deviation direction
Output publication-ready PNG images and CSV data tables
Provide automated heterogeneity interpretation based on statistical results
limitations: Requires at least 3 studies for valid heterogeneity analysis
Only supports binary, continuous, and survival data formats
R and Python must be installed in the execution environment
Does not perform subgroup analysis, meta-regression, or publication bias tests

use_cases.title: Systematic Review Heterogeneity Assessment
use_cases.description: Assess heterogeneity across clinical trials during a systematic review and identify studies that deviate from the overall effect.

use_cases.title: Biostatistical Data Visualization
use_cases.description: Visually inspect the distribution of effect sizes weighted by precision to evaluate consistency across pooled studies.

use_cases.title: Academic Thesis Meta-Analysis
use_cases.description: Generate publication-ready radial plots for a meta-analysis chapter in an epidemiology or medical research thesis.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Radial Plot Generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a radial plot from my meta-analysis data in studies.csv. The data type is binary.
prompt_templates.scenario: First-time user with a simple dataset and default settings

prompt_templates.title: Radial Plot with Specific Data Type
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a radial plot using the data in survival_data.csv with survival data type. Name the outcome Overall Survival.
prompt_templates.scenario: User specifying data type and custom outcome label

prompt_templates.title: Radial Plot with Custom Output Path
prompt_templates.prompt: Run radial plot analysis on continuous_data.csv. Data type is continuous. Output files should go to ./results/plots.
prompt_templates.scenario: User who needs output files saved to a specific directory

prompt_templates.title: Full Workflow with Validation
prompt_templates.prompt: First validate the columns in binary_trials.csv for binary meta-analysis, then generate the radial plot. Flag any studies outside the 95% confidence band and explain the heterogeneity results.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced user wanting validation before execution and detailed output interpretation

output_examples.input: Generate a radial plot from binary_trials.csv with type Binary and outcome Mortality
output_examples.output: The radial plot shows 15 studies plotted as scatter points on a precision vs standardized effect graph. A regression line through the origin represents the pooled effect. The 95% confidence band is shaded in light blue. Studies outside the band are highlighted in red. The heterogeneity statistics show I-squared of 45.2%, suggesting moderate heterogeneity. Two studies fall outside the confidence band and may be sources of heterogeneity.

output_examples.input: Create radial plot from continuous_data.csv with type Continuity
output_examples.output: 10 studies were analyzed. The standard mean difference (SMD) plot shows most points clustering within the confidence band. I-squared is 22.1% indicating low heterogeneity. The pooled SMD estimate is 0.32 [0.12; 0.52]. One study deviates from the regression line. The output includes a PNG plot and a CSV data table with precision and z-values for each study.

best_practices: Validate input data column names against the required schema before running the script
Ensure R packages meta, metafor, and ggplot2 are installed in the execution environment
Review the heterogeneity conclusion and flag any studies outside the confidence band for further analysis
anti_patterns: Running the analysis without validating that CSV columns match the expected data type schema
Using fewer than 3 studies which produces invalid heterogeneity statistics
Ignoring studies flagged outside the confidence band without considering sensitivity analysis

faq.question: What data types are supported?
faq.answer: Binary, continuous, and survival data types are supported. Each type requires specific columns in the input CSV file.

faq.question: What is a radial plot?
faq.answer: A radial plot (also called Galbraith plot) displays standardized effect sizes against precision. It helps assess heterogeneity across studies in a meta-analysis.

faq.question: What software dependencies are required?
faq.answer: R with packages meta, metafor, and ggplot2. Python with pandas, numpy, matplotlib, and scipy is used as a backup option.

faq.question: What is the minimum number of studies needed?
faq.answer: At least 3 studies are required to perform a valid heterogeneity analysis and generate a meaningful radial plot.

faq.question: What output files are generated?
faq.answer: A PNG image of the radial plot and a CSV data table containing precision, z-values, and confidence band status for each study.

faq.question: How do I interpret the heterogeneity statistics?
faq.answer: I-squared values below 25% indicate low heterogeneity, 25-50% moderate, 50-75% substantial, and above 75% high heterogeneity. Studies outside the 95% confidence band are potential sources of heterogeneity.
